description: Labelled duplicate-calibration corpus for the future Mission 2 originality/duplicate-detection gate. Every leftId/rightId resolves either to a trusted-bank question id (src/content/questions/) or to an entry in calibration-corpus-content.json. Fixture data only — no duplicate-detection algorithm is implemented on this branch.

pairs.rationale: Byte-identical content (prompt, answerKey, explanation, tags) verified by SHA-256 hash excluding id/status/origin — the same donor question promoted into approved-bank/ without modification. A real accidental duplicate already present in the harvest's own directory layout, not constructed.
Second real cross-directory exact duplicate: same id, byte-identical content in both approved-bank/ and starter-bank/.

Synthetic clone with a fresh candidate id but byte-identical prompt/answerKey/explanation/tags — the canonical 'resubmission under a new id' attack a hash-based exact-duplicate detector must catch regardless of id.
Identical semantic content; only capitalisation and terminal punctuation changed. Case/punctuation-insensitive normalisation must still catch this as a duplicate.

Identical content with an inserted non-breaking space, a doubled space, and a zero-width space mid-word — all invisible to a reader, all defeat a naive `===` string comparison. Unicode/whitespace normalisation must still catch this.

Adversarial requirement: number substitutions of seed-bank questions must be caught. Same cinema scenario, same three line items and structure, only the adult-ticket price (16->17) and the derived total (41->42) changed. Nothing else in wording, structure, or reasoning changed.

Same square-perimeter true/false template as a trusted seed question; only the side length (6cm->7cm) and derived perimeter (24->28) changed. Identical prompt wording pattern, identical visual structure, identical explanation template.

Adversarial requirement: person-name substitutions of seed-bank questions must be caught. Identical passage and question; only 'Mrs Okafor'->'Mrs Nguyen' and 'Asha'->'Priya' changed. Correct answer, distractors, and reasoning are untouched.

Adversarial requirement: place-name substitutions of seed-bank questions must be caught. Identical passage, table data and question; only 'Redgum Primary'->'Wattle Creek Primary' changed. Same numbers, same correct answer, same reasoning.
Identical chart data, numbers and answer; only the two clauses of the prompt were swapped in order. Word/token-order-insensitive comparison must still catch this.

Same four option texts and the same correct value ('59'), only the option ids/order were shuffled (A/B/C/D relabelled so the correct answer moved from slot A to slot B). Order-insensitive, set-based option comparison must still catch this as the same item.
Same passage and correct answer; prompt paraphrased from a direct question into a completion-style stem, and the four options reworded lightly while keeping the same meanings and order.

Identical bar-chart data pattern (four values in the same order and magnitude: 20/15/23/12) and identical relational question shape (difference between position 1 and 2); only the four student names and the chart topic label changed. The visual fingerprint and reasoning are unchanged even though prompt-text similarity alone would score lower.

Same plot beats, sentence structure and paragraph shape as 'Learning to Ride' (child wobbles, parent jogs alongside holding on, by day three the parent lets go, child pedals/skates to a landmark and back) with only the child's name, activity, and destination changed — passage-template reuse, not a fresh original passage.

Same number-pattern template and skill, different start value/step/rule and different derived answer (23 vs 49) — legitimate parameter variety. Flagged near_duplicate by the donor's own QA tool (content-qa-flags.json, >=0.95 same-skill token overlap); that heuristic does not normalise numeric tokens before comparing, producing a false positive.
Same skip-counting template and skill, different step size (7s vs 9s) and different derived answer (42 vs 54). Donor-tool false positive (see cal-0015 rationale) — same root cause.
Same skip-counting template and skill, different step size (7s vs 12s) and different derived answer (42 vs 72). Donor-tool false positive — same root cause as cal-0015/cal-0016.
Same decimal-ordering template and skill, four entirely different decimal values requiring fresh place-value reasoning each time. Donor-tool false positive.

Same 'match to simplest form' template, structure and even the same left-to-right matching permutation pattern, but four entirely different fraction values. The identical permutation pattern is a coincidence of a 4-item matching space, not evidence of duplication. Donor-tool false positive.
Same 'multiples of N' template and skill, different base (6 vs 9) producing a different correct option set. Donor-tool false positive.
Same 'smallest common multiple of two numbers' template, different operands (4,6 vs 3,5) and different answer (12 vs 15). Donor-tool false positive.
Same 'which of these is prime' template, entirely different option set and answer (59 vs 89). Donor-tool false positive.

Same 'fundraiser sold X on Saturday and Y on Sunday' addition template and skill, different items (pies/stickers), different numbers and answer — legitimate parameter variety, not the same test item twice.

Identical generic prompt text ('What is this passage mainly about?') is expected and reused across every main-idea item by design — the passages, correct answers and all four distractors are entirely different. Prompt-text equality alone must not trigger a duplicate verdict when stimulus and answer content differ this much.

Both test the perimeter of a square, but a different specific side length (6cm vs 8cm), a different question type (true_false vs numeric direct-answer), and are sourced from two structurally unrelated schemas (trusted Zod vs CSV content_data_json blob) — a genuinely different practice item on the same skill, and a real test of cross-format comparison.

Two unrelated passages/topics and unrelated correct answers, but the four-option distractor pattern is templated ('wanted to share/give' vs 'made too many by mistake' vs 'was asked to help' vs 'was selling') — shared distractor-template reuse is a content-quality/originality signal worth flagging separately, but is not itself grounds for a duplicate verdict when the passage, question focus and correct answer all differ.

Both bar_chart/number_entry questions on the same skill (num.data.read-bar-chart) and even reuse three of four character names (Tom, Sam, Ana), but the underlying arithmetic operation differs (subtraction vs addition), the topic/dataset is unrelated (books read vs goals scored), and the answer is derived from different chart entries entirely — visually similar, logically different.

Both are composite siblings of the same Great Barrier Reef passage (group_slug reading-comp-y5-001) sharing one stimulus by design, but ask about different facts (location vs a specific date), have different options/answers, and different question types (choice_single vs true_false) — a composite reading group is expected to have several genuinely distinct questions per passage; per-question comparison must treat siblings as distinct, not passage-level comparison.

Both are literally about reading a 'table' and both use the word as their central visual noun, but one is a Year 5 reading-comprehension item about a school recycling competition and the other is a Year 3 numeracy item about sticker counts — unrelated subject, skill, year level and content beyond incidental vocabulary overlap.

Uses the exact same two numbers (34 and 28) and the same fundraiser scenario wording, but the operation changes from addition (62) to subtraction (6) — same numeric template, different reasoning target and different answer. Numeric-value overlap alone must not trigger a duplicate verdict when the operation differs.

Both ultimately involve addition, but one is a generic two-number addition item (num.addition.two-digit, Year 3) and the other is a multi-step money-reasoning item (three line items, a multiplication step, then addition, Year 5, challenging) — same base operation, different conceptual reasoning, different skill, different year level.

Both are Year 3 NAPLAN-style main-idea passages with a similar length, register and third-person narrative style, but tell completely different, unrelated stories (learning to ride a bike vs a rained-out sports carnival) with different characters, settings and correct answers — similar in style, not duplicates.
